pi_items.source: Verified in setup_task.sh (line 11) and export_result.sh (line 13); README confirms same path (line 17)
Task description requires 'at least two linear measurements' (task.json description); README Goal section confirms 2 measurements required (lines 13-15)
README Ground Truth section states 'Each measurement has a value > 80 mm' (line 30); verifier.py defaults to 80.0 (line 30)

pi_items.verified_value: ct_cranium/0051 from GitHub InVesalius v3.0 release
pi_items.source: setup_invesalius.sh line 72-73 specifies DICOM source as 'https://github.com/invesalius/invesalius3/releases/download/v3.0/0051.zip'; README confirms 'CT Cranium (Dataset 0051)' (line 41)

README states 'Expected transverse diameter: ~140–170 mm' (line 42). Scientific literature (Anthropometric Evaluation and Thai population studies) shows adult cranial transverse breadth (eu-eu) ranges 130-142 mm. The metadata range [130, 180] incorrectly includes anteroposterior length values (166-174 mm), confusing breadth with length measurements.

README states 'Expected AP diameter: ~160–190 mm' (line 43). Scientific literature (Thai population and anthropometric studies) shows adult cranial anteroposterior length (gl-op) ranges 166-174 mm. The metadata range [150, 200] is excessively broad and extends beyond actual anatomical measurements.

privileged_info_summary: The CT Cranium dataset (0051) is a real, non-synthetic DICOM archive from InVesalius v3.0 official GitHub releases (not a synthetic dataset). The task correctly specifies an output path, requires 2 measurements, and sets a minimum measurement threshold of 80 mm. However, the expected measurement ranges in metadata are anatomically inaccurate: the transverse diameter range [130, 180] conflates breadth measurements (~130-142 mm) with anteroposterior length values (~166-174 mm), and the AP range [150, 200] is excessively broad compared to actual adult cranial anteroposterior length (166-174 mm). The README documentation provides more accurate estimates ([140, 170] for transverse, [160, 190] for AP), which are closer to scientific literature but still imperfect.
